resources.string: This is a gentle 30-day journey through loss — one short passage and reflection each day. There\'s no rush and no wrong way to grieve. Begin whenever you\'re ready.

string-array.name: grief_themes
string-array.item: The first wave
It\'s okay to mourn
Through the valley
New mercies
He heals the broken
You are held
Your tears are seen
The God of all comfort
Morning will come
The hope of resurrection
Even this
When you are empty
Through the waters
Pour out your heart
Come and rest
Speak to your soul
A weight of glory
No more tears
Take heart
Nothing can separate
Your Redeemer lives
Precious in his sight
Grief, but with hope
Where help comes from
New strength
A peace beyond
Number our days
Cast it on him
Untroubled hearts
Fullness of joy

string-array.name: grief_reflections
string-array.item: Grief comes in waves, and the first ones can knock you down. You don\'t have to be strong right now — God is near to the broken-hearted, and He is near to you.
Jesus called mourners blessed, not because grief is good, but because He meets us in it. Let yourself mourn; it is love with nowhere to go, and He sees every tear.
The valley is real, and you are walking through it — but you are not walking alone. His rod and staff are with you, even in the dark.
When the grief feels endless, His mercy is the thing that does not end. It is new every morning — enough for just today.
He does not stand far off from your pain. He draws close to bind up what is broken, gently, in His own time.
Fear can crowd in when we lose what we love. Hear Him say: do not be afraid, I am with you, I am holding you.
Nothing you feel is wasted on God. He keeps your tears in His bottle; not one of them falls unnoticed.
He is the Father of compassion and the God of all comfort — and the comfort He gives you will one day overflow to someone else.
Weeping may stay for the night, and the night may feel long. But joy is coming; He has not forgotten the morning.
Death is not the end of the story. Jesus is the resurrection and the life, and those who are His are more alive than we can imagine.
You may not see how any good could come from this. Trust that God can hold even this loss and work within it, though it is a mystery now.
When your heart and your strength fail, God is still the strength of your heart. Lean your whole weight on Him today.
The waters are deep, but they will not overwhelm you. He has promised to be with you as you pass through — not around.
You don\'t have to tidy your feelings before you come to Him. Pour out your heart — all of it — for He is a refuge.
If you are weary and heavy-laden, He is not asking you to carry more. Come to Him, and find rest for your soul.
Some days you must speak to your own soul: why are you downcast? Put your hope in God; you will praise Him again.
Your sorrow is real, and it is also not the final word. He is working an eternal weight of glory that outlasts every grief.
There is a day coming when He will wipe every tear from your eyes — no more death, no more mourning, no more pain.
Be strong, and take heart. Not because you feel strong, but because your hope is in the One who is.
Nothing — not death, not life, not this loss — can separate you from the love of God in Christ Jesus. Nothing.
With Job you can say, even through tears: I know that my Redeemer lives. Your hope is not in vain.
Your loved one is precious in the Lord\'s sight — more precious even than to you. They are safe with Him.
We do grieve — deeply — but not as those who have no hope. For those in Christ, goodbye is not forever.
When you lift your eyes and wonder where help will come from, remember: your help comes from the Maker of heaven and earth.
Those who wait on the Lord will renew their strength. You will not always feel this weak; He is renewing you.
There is a peace that doesn\'t make sense, that guards your heart when nothing else can. Ask Him for it today.
Loss teaches us to number our days and hold them gently. Let it make you tender, not bitter.
He cares for you — really cares. So cast all your anxiety on Him; you were never meant to carry it alone.
Let not your heart be troubled. The same Jesus who wept at a grave is preparing a place where there are no more graves.
The journey doesn\'t erase the loss, but it points you home — to His presence, where there is fullness of joy. Keep walking; He is with you.
